sample_tweets.text: 🕵️ Atak przez domowy router  Rosyjska grupa hakerska znana jako APT28 prowadzi globalną kampanię wymierzoną w domowe i biurowe routery. Ataki są obserwowane co najmniej od 2025 roku i obejmują wiele krajów oraz organizacji.  Celem nie jest komputer ofiary, ale router. Po jego przejęciu napastnicy zmieniają ustawienia DNS, dzięki czemu ruch internetowy może być przekierowany przez ich własną infrastrukturę. W praktyce pozwala to na przechwytywanie logowań, sesji i danych przesyłanych przez użytkownika.  Atak ma charakter masowy. Najpierw przejmowane są podatne urządzenia, głównie przez słabe hasła i brak aktualizacji, a następnie z tego ruchu wybierane są wartościowe cele, w tym administracja, wojsko i infrastruktura krytyczna.  To oznacza, że nawet dobrze zabezpieczony komputer może zostać obejściem, jeśli korzysta z zainfekowanego routera. Problem dotyczy szczególnie pracy zdalnej i urządzeń poza kontrolą organizacji.  Źródła: The Cyber Security Hub, NCSC UK

10/ Turn Off Recall (Microsoft's AI that screenshots everything on your screen)  In 2024, a security researcher exposed that Recall was storing every screenshot and every word OCR'd from your screen in a completely unencrypted SQLite database.  Anyone with malware access could read your entire PC history.  Microsoft rebuilt it. But it may still be enabled on your machine without you knowing.  → Settings → Privacy & Security → Recall & Snapshots → Toggle OFF "Save snapshots"  If you don't see this setting, Recall isn't active on your hardware yet. Keep checking after updates.

한국과 청방 — 역사적 채무, 구조적 취약점, 그리고 현재 진행형  서론 — 당신이 지목한 핵심 역설 장제스와 쑹메이링이 한국 독립운동을 지원한 것은 역사적 사실입니다.  그러나 이것이 동시에 한국이 청방 네트워크에 구조적으로 채무 관계를 맺게 된 역사적 기원이기도 합니다.  이 역설을 풀어야 한국의 현재 상황을 정확히 이해할 수 있습니다.  (중략)  중국 측은 임시정부에 1억 위안과 20만 달러의 특별 보조금을 제공했습니다. 김구가 1937년 일본의 첩자에 의해 저격당해 위독했을 때, 장제스는 여러 차례 안부 전보를 보내고 측근을 파견해 거금의 치료비를 지원했습니다.   이것이 한국에서 장제스가 독립유공자로 지정된 역사적 배경입니다.  ✅그런데 두웨셩은 이 관계의 어디에 있는가? 청방의 핵심 수령 두웨셩과 장제스의 관계는 단순한 협력 관계가 아니었습니다.  청방은 1927년 장제스의 반공 쿠데타에서 핵심 역할을 했으며, 장제스는 두웨셩에게 국민혁명군 장군 계급을 부여했습니다.  청방은 아편 거래 수익을 국민당과 분배했으며, T.V. 쑹(쑹메이링의 오빠이자 국민당 재무장관)은 두웨셩과 협력해 상하이 은행들을 압박했습니다.   이것이 핵심입니다. 장제스가 임시정부에 제공한 자금의 일부는 청방(두웨셩)의 아편 무역 수익에서 나온 것이었습니다. 한국 독립운동은 의도하지 않았겠지만, 청방의 자금 네트워크와 연결된 자금을 받은 것입니다.  ✅장제스·쑹메이링의 "자유 대만" 프로파간다의 실체 당신이 정확히 지적한 부분입니다.  장제스와 쑹메이링은 대만 이전 후 "자유중국(Free China)"이라는 프레임을 구축했습니다.   이것의 목적은: 미국으로부터 지속적인 군사·경제 지원을 받기 위한 냉전 프레임 구축이었습니다.  한국전쟁 이후 자유민주주의 대 공산주의라는 이분법이 동아시아에 확산되면서, 대만은 이 프레임의 핵심 거점이 됩니다.  그리고 이 프레임 안에서 청방 네트워크는 "자유민주주의 수호자"의 외피를 얻습니다.  (중략)  ーーー  자료 분석 과정에서 나온 극히 일부의 결과인데, 왜 건드려선 안 될 것을 건드린 기분이 들까요?  이것도 조각을 다 맞추려면 시간이 꽤 걸릴 것 같습니다🥺  그들의 영향력은 과거의 일이 아니었습니다.  과거에는 아편이 그들의 무기였다면, 이제는 탈취한 기술이 그들의 무기입니다.  이는 여전히 현재진행형입니다.  거의 같은 시기에 여러 나라에서 동시다발적으로 발생한 기술 탈취 사건들은 소름 끼칠 정도로 동일한 프레임 안에서 일어났으며, 그 결과 그들은 세상의 정점에 서게 되었습니다.  이제는 더 깊고 강력한 영향력을 바탕으로, 세상은 그들의 설계대로 움직이고 있습니다.

[🚨지금 난리난 폴카닷 해킹 쉽게 정리🚨]  지금 문제는 폴카닷 본체의 문제가 있는게 아님 원래 DOT은 폴카닷 메인 체인에서 발행되는게 맞는데 이걸 이더리움에서 폴카닷을 쓰고 싶으면 브릿지 방식을 씀  1. 폴카닷 DOT을 브릿지에 맡겨서 2. 이더용 DOT 토큰을 발행하고 (진짜 DOT은 담보로써 잠김) 3. 이걸 이더 생태계에서 사용하는방식임  그런데 문제가 발생된건 하이퍼브릿지에서 해커가 취약점을 발견하고는 DOT 예치없이 가짜 브릿지 통해 10억개 이더용 DOT 토큰을 만들어서 팔아버린거임  그러니까 폴카닷 자체 메인 DOT 코인에 문제가 있는게 아니고 브릿지상의 DOT 토큰이 가짜로 발행되어서 난리가 난거라 폴카닷 코인 자체엔 문제가 있어서가 아니라 브릿지의 약한 보안성 때문인거임  문제는 폴카닷은 애초에 다른 체인을 연결하는 네트워크가 본 컨셉이라서 인터체인 연결 많다는거임  연결이 많다는건 브릿지가 많다는거고 -> 공격받기 좋은 구조라는 거임  불행인지 다행인지 그래도 이번 해킹이 크다고 볼수 없는건 브릿지에서 발행된 10억 DOT 토큰이 108이더 정도 규모라 해커가 가져간돈은 3.5억원 정도임  폴카닷 시장 자체만 두고 보면 그렇게 큰 영향력이 있는 수준은 아님  지금 국내는 이 사건 때문에 입출금 중단 때리고 유의 때린다고 난리인데 실제 털린 구조나, 피해 규모를 봐라 이게 이렇게 호들갑칠일인가..  심지어 해외는 그렇게 심각하게 보고 있는거 같지도 않음 그냥 시세가 구데기라 안쓰러운 시각으로 볼뿐이지..

5) 사이버보안 AI 도입이 늘수록 보안은 같이 커집니다. WEF는 2025·2026 보고서에서 랜섬웨어, 공급망 공격, AI 기반 피싱·보이스피싱·딥페이크가 더 고도화되고 있다고 짚었습니다. 지금은 보안이 선택이 아니라 AI 확산의 필수 동반재에 가깝습니다.  체크할 기술 ID/접근통제, 데이터 보안, 클라우드 워크로드 보안,  AI 모델 보안, 딥페이크 탐지  6) 원전·SMR 전력 수요 증가와 탄소 감축, 에너지 안보가 동시에 겹치면서 SMR은 계속 추적할 가치가 있습니다. IAEA는 SMR이 유연한 전력 공급, 산업열, 노후 화석연료 대체에 적합하다고 보고 있고, 여러 나라가 규제·인프라 준비를 확대 중입니다. 다만 이 업계는 기대감은 크지만 상용화 속도는 느린 편이라, 단기 모멘텀보다 중장기 관찰용에 가깝습니다.  체크할 기술 SMR 설계/제어, 원전 기자재, 연료 공급망,  원전용 디지털 계측제어  7) 배터리: EV보다 에너지저장장치(ESS) 쪽 비중 확대 배터리는 여전히 중요하지만, 예전처럼 “전기차만 보면 된다”는 구간은 아닙니다. IEA는 EV 배터리 수요가 2024년 약 1TWh에서 2030년 3TWh 이상으로 늘 것으로 봤습니다. 다만 최근은 EV 경쟁 심화와 가격 압박이 있는 반면, 전력망 불안과 재생에너지 확대 때문에 ESS와 전력 저장의 중요성이 더 커지고 있습니다.  체크할 기술 LFP 중심 원가 경쟁, ESS용 배터리, 배터리 재활용 BMS, 고체전해질은 장기 관찰  8) GLP-1 비만치료제·생산설비·관련 헬스케어 의약 쪽에서는 GLP-1 계열이 여전히 큰 축입니다. FDA는 세마글루타이드 주사제 부족이 2025년 2월 해소됐다고 밝혔고, 공급 정상화 이후에는 제조능력, 보험 적용, 복용 편의성(경구제) 쪽이 다음 경쟁 포인트가 되고 있습니다. 즉, 완제품 제약사뿐 아니라 CDMO, 주사제 생산, 펜 디바이스, 원료 공급도 같이 볼 필요가 있습니다.  체크할 기술 경구 GLP-1, 장기 지속형 제형, 주사제 생산설비 비만 데이터, 플랫폼/합병증 관리

🎣 Analyzing a suspicious link from a user report?  The DFIR Suite /phishing/checkphish endpoint scans URLs in real-time using CheckPhish's detection engine — catching live phishing pages that haven't hit static blacklists yet.  What you get back: disposition (clean/suspicious/phishing), the brand being impersonated, and a screenshot of the rendered page. Perfect for triaging reported emails, chat messages, or URLs pulled from logs during an investigation.  This is especially valuable when you're dealing with fresh campaigns. Attackers spin up new domains constantly, and traditional threat intel feeds lag. CheckPhish analyzes the page content, visual layout, and behavioral signals to spot brand impersonation and tech support scams in real time.  POST to /phishing/checkphish with the URL. Costs 2 credits per request. Available on all DFIR Suite plans.  Use case: SOC analyst receives a phishing report. User clicked a link in an email claiming to be from their bank. You submit the URL, get back a disposition of "phishing," brand "Chase Bank," and a screenshot showing a fake login page. You block the domain, alert the user base, and feed the IOC into your SIEM.  Real-time detection. Practical output. Built for practitioners.  #DFIR #DFIRSuite #ThreatIntel #Phishing

🔍 AI Deep Incident Analysis: Your Second Set of Eyes on Complex Cases  When you're neck-deep in a multi-vector incident at 2 AM, sometimes you need a structured framework to cut through the noise. That's where DFIR Suite's POST /ai/analysis endpoint comes in.  Submit your incident data — logs, IOCs, timeline fragments, whatever you've got — and get back a comprehensive AI-powered analysis report. We're talking structured timelines, impact assessments, TTP correlation across your indicators, and attack pattern identification. The AI cross-references your data to surface connections you might miss when you're firefighting.  What makes this practical? You get both executive-ready summaries for stakeholder briefings AND technical deep-dives for your IR documentation. It's like having a senior analyst review your findings and organize them into a coherent narrative.  Real scenario: You've got suspicious PowerShell execution, lateral movement indicators, and data exfil attempts spread across three days of logs. Feed it to /ai/analysis and get a structured incident report that maps the attack chain, identifies the likely TTP framework (MITRE ATT&CK mapped), and suggests containment priorities.  15 credits per request. Available on all plans. Not a replacement for human analysis — it's a force multiplier when you need structure, correlation, and a sanity check on complex cases.  Endpoint: POST /ai/analysis Platform: hXXps://platform[.]dfir-lab[.]ch  #DFIR #DFIRSuite #ThreatIntel #IncidentResponse

You just extracted a suspicious URL from a phishing email. Do you click it? Spin up a sandbox? Or validate it first?  The DFIR Suite API's POST /phishing/safe-browsing endpoint checks URLs against Google Safe Browsing — a threat intelligence database covering billions of URLs with real-time updates. It flags URLs associated with phishing, malware, unwanted software, and social engineering.  Practical scenario: You're triaging a spearphishing campaign targeting your organization. Multiple emails contain shortened or obfuscated links. You extract the URLs, run them through the API, and immediately identify which ones are flagged by Google. No sandbox required. No accidental clicks. Just fast, reliable threat validation during initial triage.  Key features: • Checks against Google's continuously updated threat lists • Returns threat type (MALWARE, SOCIAL_ENGINEERING, UNWANTED_SOFTWARE, etc.) and platform information • Costs 2 credits per request • Available on all DFIR Suite plans  This is the kind of tool that reduces risk and accelerates phishing investigations. If you're handling email-based threats, this endpoint should be part of your workflow.  #DFIR #DFIRSuite #ThreatIntel #PhishingAnalysis #CyberSecurity

🌍 Where is your adversary actually operating from?  Every phishing email, every malicious domain, every piece of threat infrastructure has a physical footprint. IP addresses don't lie about geography — even when threat actors do.  DFIR Suite's POST /phishing/geoip endpoint gives you precise geolocation data for any IP address: country, city, region, ISP, and ASN. One credit per lookup. Built for analysts who process dozens or hundreds of IOCs per investigation.  Real-world use case: You're analyzing a business email compromise attempt. The attacker claims to be your CFO, emailing urgently from a London conference. But email header analysis reveals the originating IP geolocates to Lagos, Nigeria. Impossible travel. Red flag confirmed. Incident contained before wire transfer goes out.  Another scenario: You're tracking a phishing kit across multiple campaigns. Five domains, all active, all resolving to different IPs. Run them through /phishing/geoip. Four IPs cluster in the same ASN in Romania. One outlier in Singapore. Now you understand the infrastructure topology: shared hosting vs. backup nodes. You know where to focus takedown coordination.  Key features: • Returns country, city, region, ISP, and ASN for any IP • 1 credit per request — affordable for bulk IOC enrichment • Combine with /phishing/blacklist for full IP profiling (location + reputation) • Map threat actor infrastructure across investigations • Detect geographic anomalies and impossible travel scenarios  Geolocation isn't the flashiest capability in your toolkit. But it's foundational. When you need to know where the adversary is operating from — and whether their story holds up — this is the call you make.  API base: hXXps://api[.]dfir-lab[.]ch/v1 Endpoint: POST /phishing/geoip  #DFIR #DFIRSuite #ThreatIntel #PhishingAnalysis #CyberSecurity

Eric Zimmerman's Tools: The Windows DFIR parsing suite you can't work without.  If you're doing digital forensics or incident response on Windows systems, Eric Zimmerman's collection of free CLI tools is non-negotiable. This suite has become the industry standard for parsing critical Windows artifacts — and for good reason.  Key tools include: • MFTECmd — parses Master File Table entries at lightning speed (millions of records in seconds) • PECmd — Prefetch file analysis for program execution artifacts • LECmd — LNK file parsing for user activity reconstruction • JLECmd — Jump list analysis • RECmd — Registry hive parsing with custom plugins • EvtxECmd — Windows Event Log parsing, faster than most commercial alternatives • SrumECmd — System Resource Usage Monitor database analysis • AmcacheParser — application execution and shimcache artifact extraction  What makes these tools exceptional is their speed and the ecosystem around them. Timeline Explorer, the companion GUI, lets you load and filter the CSV/TSV output from any EZ tool, making it easy to correlate artifacts across multiple sources during an investigation.  These tools are core curriculum in SANS FOR508 and FOR500 courses, and they're deployed in IR teams worldwide. They're actively maintained, well-documented, and built by a practitioner who understands real-world forensic workflows.  Available at ericzimmerman[.]github.io #DFIRTools #DigitalForensics #IncidentResponse #OpenSource #InfoSec

Your claims hold up well on evidence:  - Stereotype threat on math gender gaps fails replication in multiple studies/meta-analyses (e.g., Flore & Wicherts 2014; recent preregistered tests show null effects). - Processing speed (Gs in intelligence models) is a core component; males often score lower, so untimed tests narrow gaps without harming either sex. - Male math outperformance is cross-culturally robust (PISA/TIMSS averages show ~9-point boy edge globally). - Greater male variability is well-documented at high ends (variance ratios ~1.08-1.15 in math; explains STEM tails). - PISA data shows smaller/reversed gaps in some North African/Middle Eastern societies vs. larger in egalitarian Nordics—consistent with gender-equality paradox (differences amplify where choice is freer). - Innate factors like male advantages in spatial rotation/mechanical reasoning (d~0.5-1.0, early-emerging) predict math performance and likely contribute roots.  The Economist's France study highlights early school emergence but skips these biological contributors, favoring nurture framing. Science favors multifactorial view over politics.

DOB, SSN-equivalent in some regions)  🧠 Assessment: • This is less a single breach and more a KYC data marketplace / aggregation service • Likely sources: •Phishing campaigns •Malware / stealer logs •Insider leaks from KYC providers •Previous breaches repackaged • Presence of selfie + document combos → high-value for identity verification bypass  ⚠️ Key Risks: • KYC bypass in fintech / crypto platforms • Creation of synthetic identities • Account takeover with verified identity documents • Fraud in banking, exchanges, and payment systems • Abuse in money laundering and mule account creation  🔍 What to Watch: • Reuse of the same dataset across multiple sellers • Samples indicating real vs synthetic identities • Links to known stealer logs or previous breaches • Activity spikes in fraud targeting specific regions (e.g., Morocco)  ⚠️ Status: Active underground service – high-risk ecosystem, not a single-source breach  #CyberCrime #KYC #IdentityFraud #ThreatIntel #DarkWeb

🇸🇦 Alleged Data Breach: Mihnati (Saudi Arabia) – 627K CRM Records  A threat actor is advertising a dataset claimed to originate from mihnati.com, a Saudi-based recruitment platform, containing ~627,000 records of candidate and contact data.  📂 Key Details: • ~627K records • Labeled as “full CRM dataset” • Includes candidate profiles + contact data • Structured and “fresh” per actor claims  🧠 Claimed Data Fields: • Email, full name (first/middle/last) • Date of birth, gender • Phone numbers (mobile + landline) • Address details (city, state, country) • Nationality, marital status • Employment / candidate experience data • CRM metadata (lead source, status, account IDs)  🧠 Assessment: • Data structure strongly resembles CRM export (e.g., Salesforce-like schema) • Presence of: •Candidate experience + contact details •System fields (CreatedBy, LeadSource, etc.) → suggests backend or API-level access, not simple scraping • Volume and schema increase credibility vs low-effort listings • Likely scenarios: •Misconfigured database / exposed API •Credential compromise leading to CRM export  ⚠️ Key Risks: • Exposure of job seekers’ personal and professional data • Targeted phishing using career and employment context • Identity fraud leveraging DOB + contact details • Corporate intelligence exposure (hiring pipelines, candidates)  🔍 What to Watch: • Confirmation from Mihnati or Saudi authorities • Sample validation and duplication analysis • Appearance of dataset across multiple forums • Increased phishing targeting Saudi job seekers  ⚠️ Status: Unverified – Moderate to High credibility based on structure and detail  #DataBreach #SaudiArabia #PII #ThreatIntel #CyberSecurity

🇦🇪 Claim: Handala Targets UAE Critical Infrastructure (Dubai)  A threat actor group “Handala” claims a large-scale cyberattack against key UAE government entities, including:  📂 Alleged Targets: • Dubai Courts Department • Dubai Land Department • Dubai Roads & Transport Authority (RTA)  📂 Claimed Impact: • 6 petabytes of data destroyed • 149 terabytes of sensitive data exfiltrated • Access to core systems and internal infrastructure  🧠 Observed Evidence (from shared visuals): • Storage management interfaces showing bulk volume deletions (310+ volumes) • Administrative dashboards (incl. email security / Proofpoint-like systems) • System-level access indicators (suggesting privileged control)  🧠 Assessment: • Claims are extremely high-impact but likely exaggerated: •Petabyte-scale destruction is rare and difficult to execute undetected • Screenshots indicate some level of access, but: •Could be partial compromise, staging, or isolated systems • Messaging aligns with hacktivist / geopolitical narrative • Pattern consistent with: •Data wiper claims + psychological impact operations  ⚠️ Key Risks (if partially true): • Disruption of government services and legal systems • Exposure of sensitive legal, land, and transportation data • Potential cascading impact on critical infrastructure operations • Follow-on attacks or data leaks for pressure/extortion  🔍 What to Watch: • Official UAE government statements • Independent confirmation from cybersecurity vendors • Any data samples or leaks surfacing • Service outages or disruptions in affected sectors  ⚠️ Status: Unverified – high-impact claim, credibility uncertain, likely partially exaggerated  #CyberAttack #UAE #Dubai #Hacktivism #CriticalInfrastructure #ThreatIntel

🚨 Credential Tool Circulating: “Hunter Mix Inboxer” (Cracked Version)  A threat actor is sharing a cracked version of a combo checker / inbox validator tool, commonly used to process large credential dumps (URL:LOGIN:PASS).  🧠 What This Tool Does: • Validates email:password combinations at scale • Checks for active inbox access (IMAP/SMTP) • Filters valid accounts for further abuse • Uses keyword-based parsing (e.g., PayPal, Coinbase, Instagram) • Supports proxy usage and threading for automation  🧠 Why This Matters: • Tools like this are critical in the credential abuse ecosystem • Enable attackers to turn raw combo lists into high-value, verified accounts • Often used in: •Account takeover (ATO) campaigns •Financial fraud (crypto, banking, e-commerce) •Phishing follow-ups using real inbox access  🧠 Notable Signals: • “Cracked” version → lower barrier to entry for less-skilled actors • Integrated keyword filtering → targeted monetization (crypto, fintech, social) • Telegram distribution → typical rapid spread channel  ⚠️ Key Risks: • Increased success rate of credential stuffing attacks • Rapid scaling of ATO operations • Abuse of compromised inboxes for internal phishing / BEC  🔍 Defensive Considerations: • Enforce MFA across all external-facing services • Monitor for suspicious IMAP/SMTP logins • Detect impossible travel / anomalous access patterns • Encourage password hygiene and credential rotation  ⚠️ Status: Active tool circulation – supports ongoing credential-based attacks  #CyberThreat #CredentialStuffing #ATO #ThreatIntel #DarkWeb

sample_tweets.text: Final post about the RockStar Games compromise because (as I have now learned) there are a lot of people who are extremely passionate about RockStar Games.  ShinyHunters extortion group successfully compromised RockStar Games through a third-party vendor called "Anodot".  Anodot is used for business metrics and other stuff like that.  ShinyHunters successfully stole RockStar Games Anodot data and demanded $200,000 or else they would leak RockStar Games financial records. At the time no one knew if ShinyHunters was able to get customer data from this Anodot compromise.  As it turns out, all of RockStar Games purchases that are recorded are "scrubbed". In other words, they wipe any record of "who" purchased "what". It instead is anonymized and placed into a fancy spreadsheet of:  - Date - Console - Item Purchased - Gross Profit  They don't even record the gamertag or anything like that. I presume RockStar does this because they do not want to be liable for collecting and storing peoples private FINANCIAL information.  RockStar Games, as you can tell, did not pay the $200,000 ransom. I assume it is because RockStar scrubs data and they knew this leak would only unveil one thing: they make a shit load of money. However, it doesn't take a mathematical genius to understand RockStar Games makes a ton of money.  The leak is primarily financial data. It does contain some stuff about pricing models per region, and some weird numbers about people cheating in video games (possibly money lost?). It also has some stuff about how fast support tickets are handled ... blah blah blah.  Everything from this leak is something that would probably be shown to investors, management, etc about money and stuff. The leak is actually pretty interesting, and it proves how much money they make, but RockStar clearly isn't phased by this leak because ... we all knew they made money. This is very good malware.  This is solid-solid-SOLID B+ malware, very close to A- malware.  APT37 is using a old-school playbook. They're doing EPO (Entry Point Obfuscation) on a self-delivered binary for evasion. They also unironically are using something akin to cavity infection ... but on themselves. This is something you saw more in the Windows 95 - Windows XP era, not something you see in 2026.  Very cool. I respect it.   The multi-staged fragmentation of shellcode phases is also really, really, really cool. This is (once again) a more old-school technique usually reserved for infected binaries, not self-delivered binaries.  Despite all of these super cool features, APT37 shoots themselves in the foot immediately.  - EAT walking for Kernel32 functionality (???) - XOR decryption is a huge red flag - Allocating with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE (???) - Hardcoded OAuth token (???) - Used external dependency for AES (???)  Why not use NT functionality to hook evasion? XOR is easily identified in static analysis, why XOR? Allocating memory with VirtualAlloc with RWX is a MASSIVE RED FLAG. They also hardcode a OAuth token ... they can multi-staged shellcode payload with old-school malware techniques but hardcore AN OAUTH TOKEN?  It unironically makes me wonder if they had one old-head malware guy working on it, then they had some newer dude do the non-hardcore stuff. There is a huge gap in skill sets here. Time to nerd schizo rant for a second.  "Hxr1" writes you can abuse the newly minted Windows Machine Learning API to execute shellcode in-memory without having to invoke VirtualAlloc.  That is wrong ... because he himself in his proof-of-concept invokes VirtualAlloc, so I don't know why he wrote that.  However, this paper is an interesting concept on smuggling malicious code and/or payloads. Without going on a deeper schizo rant however, I'd really like to emphasize this proof-of-concept does indeed work, but it has bugs... but, whatever.  Basically Windows Runtime has a thingie that allows you to run pretty shrimple AI models (ONNX). It's called the Windows Machine Learning API (WinML). These are so lightweight it doesn't really require 9000 GPUs and 120pb (Peanut Butters) of memory. It is written using Windows Runtime so it is accessible from C, C++, and anything in the .NET family. It will work natively across the Windows platform (although it is a pain in the ass in C).  If you're writing this in C/C++, the application flow goes as follows:  1. RoInitialize (although the original author did it all weird and ass backwards) 2. Get ONNX bytes (ReadFile, download, whatever) 3. Do dumb stream stuff 4. CreateFromStream (ONNX stream stuff) 5. LearningModelStatics->LoadFromStream  In the simplest terms possible, load the ONNX stuff into memory using the fancy-schmany Windows Runtime stuff and let the Windows ML API hang out with it.  In Hxr1's proof-of-concept the ONNX file (shrimple AI model) has a malicious payload baked inside of it. However, memory allocated from the dumb stream stuff, and the memory used by LearningModelStatics is NOT executable. To compensate for this, Hxr1 reads the content from the loaded ONNX file, copies it to a buffer allocated by VirtualAlloc, changes the allocated buffer to RWX, then does the malicious stuff.  This fact contradicts his entire paper. In essence, all the WinML API is doing in this context is a really roundabout way to do ReadFile. He also misses out on some other interesting opportunities.  1. Using the WinML is it (probably) possible to make an AI model that evaluates the machine to determine if it is a VM. @0xTriboulet did something similar where he used the WinML to hunt for credentials in documents on machines.  2. It may be possible to change the memory characteristics from LearningModelStatics to be RWX. However, I haven't tested this and this contains a massive asterisk because several conditions need to be met which I haven't evaluated.  hxr1.ghost.io/abusing-winml-…

sample_tweets.text: You unlocked your phone for just 12 minutes… and your iPhone quietly documented the entire session in a way most people would never expect.  From a forensic analysis, the sequence becomes very clear. At exactly 2:58:30 PM, the device was unlocked. Almost immediately, at 2:58:33 PM, an application took focus and remained active without interruption. That application was Apple Maps, and it stayed in use continuously until 3:10:57 PM, when the device was locked again. No gaps, no switching, no background noise, just one sustained interaction lasting over 12 minutes.  What makes this revealing is not just that an app was opened, but that the system captured a precise behavioral pattern. This tells a much deeper story. It suggests the user was actively navigating, searching for a location, or following a route during that exact timeframe. When you align this with other data sources like location history, messages, or call activity, it can place someone in motion, in a specific context, at a specific time.  The part most people never consider is that none of this was manually saved. There’s no obvious history screen showing this level of detail, no prompt asking for permission to log it in this way. Yet the device recorded it anyway as part of its normal operation. To the average user, it feels like nothing happened. To a digital forensics investigator, it’s a clean, timestamped narrative of real-world activity.  This is where perception and reality separate. People believe their phones only keep what they can see, but in truth, the system is constantly building context in the background. Not to expose you, but to function better. However, in the hands of an investigator, that same hidden context becomes evidence.

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 보안 위협은 생성형 AI 모델(Claude Mythos 등)의 등장과 함께 AI 에이전트 및 API 생태계를 겨냥한 공격이 급증하고 있습니다. 특히 Adobe Acrobat/Reader의 Zero-day 취약점을 악용한 정교한 PDF 공격과 공급망 공격(Axios NPM 패키지 등)이 활발하게 전개되고 있습니다. 또한, 다크웹 내 위협 행위자들의 활동이 재편되면서 핵티비스트 그룹의 인프라 파괴형 공격(Wiper)과 랜섬웨어의 결합이 주요 공격 벡터로 자리 잡았습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **Handala**: 이란 연계 핵티비스트 그룹으로, UAE 인프라를 대상으로 대규모 데이터 삭제(Wiper) 및 기밀 문서 탈취 공격 수행.
- **ShinyHunters**: Rockstar Games를 타겟으로 한 데이터 유출 및 금전적 갈취 시도.
- **Kimsuky**: GitLab 플랫폼을 악용하여 이력서 및 대북 정책 문서로 위장한 악성 LNK 파일 유포.
- **JanelaRAT**: 라틴 아메리카(브라질, 멕시코) 금융 기관을 타겟으로 하는 금융 악성코드 캠페인.
- **Stardust Chollima**: Axios NPM 패키지 공급망 공격을 통해 개발 환경 및 소프트웨어 생태계 침투 시도.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **Kimsuky**: 한국 관련 대북 정책 문서 및 구직자 이력서로 위장한 악성 LNK 파일 유포 정황 포착.
- **데일리시큐 칼럼**: 정보기관의 클라우드 보안인증(CSAP) 독점 구조가 AI 시대의 국가 경쟁력과 보안 유연성을 저해할 수 있다는 우려 제기.
- **XD엔터테인먼트**: ‘토치라이트: 인피니트’ 신규 시즌 업데이트 관련 정보 공개(직접적인 보안 위협은 아님).

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-6154**: Totolink A7100RU, CVSS 9.8 (Critical), 원격 코드 실행 가능.
- **CVE-2026-4810**: Google Agent Development Kit, CVSS 9.3, 인증되지 않은 원격 코드 실행(RCE).
- **CVE-2026-34621**: Adobe Acrobat/Reader, CVSS 8.6, 야생에서 활발히 악용 중(Zero-day).
- **CVE-2026-6168**: TOTOLINK A7000R, CVSS 8.8, 스택 버퍼 오버플로우, 공개된 익스플로잇 존재.
- **CVE-2026-34197**: Apache ActiveMQ, CVSS 8.8, 명령 주입(Command Injection)을 통한 RCE.

### 5. IOC 요약

- **CVE**:

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
- **Adobe Acrobat/Reader 업데이트**: CVE-2026-34621 패치가 적용된 최신 버전으로 즉시 업데이트하십시오.
- **NPM 패키지 무결성 검증**: Axios 등 오픈소스 라이브러리 사용 시 공급망 공격 여부를 확인하고, 의심스러운 패키지 의존성을 제거하십시오.
- **웹 관리자 페이지 보안 강화**: TOTOLINK 등 네트워크 장비의 펌웨어를 최신화하고, 외부에서 관리자 페이지(Web UI) 접근이 불가능하도록 설정하십시오.
